metadata.title: Claude, Peer-Reviewed.
metadata.topic: CODING AGENTS IN THE SOCIAL SCIENCES
metadata.description_blurb: 1,260 quantitative social scientists. 81% have tried AI. 20% let it run the analysis. That 20% is posting 73% more working papers — and the gap runs along exactly the fault lines you'd fear: economists vs. education researchers, PhD students vs. full professors, men at more than twice the rate of women. Liam, in for Bear.
metadata.source_chapter: Anthropic, 'Coding agents in the social sciences' (May 27, 2026) — Lyttelton, Massenkoff, Wilmers
metadata.source: Anthropic, 'Coding agents in the social sciences' (May 27, 2026). Lyttelton, Massenkoff, Wilmers. Survey of 1,260 quantitative social scientists, Feb–Mar 2026.

beats.narration_text: Hej, Liam — this is Liam, in for Bear. Four out of five social scientists have tried AI in their research. One in five lets it run the analysis. That one in five is pulling ahead — and it's not who you'd hope.

props.command: Survey 1,260 social scientists on AI and coding agents. Map adoption by discipline, career stage, and gender. Measure working paper output.

beats.narration_text: First, the distinction that carries the whole report. A chatbot is a suggestion machine. You ask it to help with code; it writes some; you run it; you come back. The loop is yours. A coding agent takes a research idea and a dataset, writes the analysis, runs it, reads the output, iterates, and hands you the result. What was irreducibly human in empirical research — the judgment call at every step of the analysis — is, for the first time, automatable. Among the researchers who've made the switch, Claude Code dominates: 86% use it, with Codex next at 31%.

Adoption breaks hard by discipline. Economics is at 38% — nearly two in five. Education sits at 4%. That's a ten-fold difference. And this isn't just an AI gap — the AI-use numbers for both fields are close; Communication researchers use AI at 86%. But using AI and letting it run the analysis are different things. Communication: 86% AI, 6% coding agents. The gap between the two dots on each row IS the story.

props.sparkLine: Everyone uses AI. Not everyone lets it run.

beats.narration_text: The who is just as stark. PhD students adopt at 27%; full professors at 9%. That's adoption dropping by more than half at tenure. And the gender gap is the number you should sit with: researchers with typically male names adopt coding agents at 22%; typically female names, 9%. More than double. For context, the AI use numbers are 81% versus 78% — a 3-point gap. The agent era turns a 3-point gap into a 13-point gap. All of these differences are statistically significant, and all of them are starker than the equivalent gaps in general AI use. The agent era is opening more unequal than the chatbot era.

props.sparkLine: The agent era is opening more unequal than the chatbot era.

beats.narration_text: What are they actually doing with it? Not what the discourse fears. The discourse fears AI-written papers — slop flooding the journals. The data shows AI-written code. 97% of coding agent users generate analysis code. Only about a third of all AI users have drafted prose at all. Editing beats drafting everywhere. Only economists and management researchers commonly draft new text. The headline finding is boring and important: this is a coding tool.

props.sparkLine: The adoption is about code. Not prose.

beats.narration_text: Now the centerpiece. Agent users versus non-users — same discipline, same career stage — how much more output are they posting? Empirical projects started: plus 9%. Grant proposals: plus 33%. Working papers: plus 73%. Then the ladder stops. Journal submissions: plus 3%. Resubmissions: minus 6%. Statistically nothing. There are two honest readings. One: agents are new, and papers take years to reach journals — come back in 2027. Two: agents are genuinely good at getting projects running and no help at all perfecting the last mile. And the standing caveat, stated plainly: this is descriptive, not causal. Early adopters may simply be the already-productive.

props.sparkLine: Agents launch projects. They don't finish papers.

beats.narration_text: The expectations data is the most interesting chart in the report. Researchers are highly optimistic that AI will help them personally publish more — 88% above the midpoint, half at 8 or higher on a 10-point scale. And optimism rises with use: every additional use case adopted pushes the line up. But 70% are more optimistic about their own paper productivity than about the field as a whole. The gap never closes. They believe it will help them publish. And they suspect the flood might hurt the science — congestion, attention competition, selective reporting at scale. Good for my papers. Unclear for my science.

props.sparkLine: Good for my papers. Unclear for my science.

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. This survey is the baseline of a randomized experiment — Anthropic is handing researchers Claude Code and measuring what happens. Results pending. Meanwhile the way we study the economy already runs, in part, through analysis decisions made by AI. The caveats matter: 1,260 self-selected respondents, recruited with Claude Max access as an incentive. Output quantity measured; quality not. Descriptive throughout.

props.title: Claude, Peer-Reviewed.
artifactLines: 81% of social scientists tried AI; 20% use coding agents that write and run code.
Adoption splits hard by discipline: economics 38%, education 4%. Gender gap: 22% vs 9%.
Agent users post 73% more working papers — journal submissions are statistically flat.
The discourse fears AI prose; the adoption is about code: 97% use it for analysis.
Researchers are optimistic about their own output and worried about their field.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Here's my research question and a description of my dataset. Plan the full analysis pipeline, then split it honestly: which steps could you run autonomously as a coding agent, and which decisions should stay mine? Paste that into Claude, describe your actual project, and watch where it draws the line. The honest answer is probably more collaborative than either extreme.

props.command: Here's my research question and a description of my dataset. Plan the full analysis pipeline, then split it honestly: which steps could you run autonomously as a coding agent, and which decisions should stay mine?

beats.narration_text: Claude, Peer-Reviewed. Liam, in for Bear.
